theorem_title: Existence of light vertex sets in weighted graphs
theorem_statement:
Let \(G=(V,E,w)\) be a weighted graph on \(n\) vertices, and let \(L\) be its weighted graph Laplacian,
\[
L=\sum_{(s,t)\in E} w(s,t)(\delta_s-\delta_t)(\delta_s-\delta_t)^T.
\]
For \(S\subseteq V\), let \(L_S\) be the Laplacian of the graph obtained by keeping only edges internal to \(S\). For \(0<\varepsilon<1\), there exists a subset \(S\subseteq V\) such that
\[
|S|\ge \frac{\varepsilon n}{42}
\]
and
\[
L_S\preceq \varepsilon L.
\]
raw_proof_text:
The proof is a greedy spectral construction. Let \(L^\dagger\) be the Moore--Penrose pseudoinverse of \(L\), and let
\[
M=L^{\dagger/2}.
\]
Conjugating by \(M\), it is enough to control
\[
M L_S M.
\]
Indeed, the inequality \(L_S\preceq \varepsilon L\) is equivalent on the image of \(L\) to
\[
M L_S M\preceq \varepsilon P,
\]
where \(P=MLM\) is the orthogonal projection onto the image of \(L\). Thus the goal is to build \(S\) so that the largest eigenvalue of the normalized internal-edge Laplacian \(M L_S M\) is at most \(\varepsilon\).

For an edge \(e=(s,t)\), define its leverage score by
\[
\tau(e)=w(s,t)(\delta_s-\delta_t)^T L^\dagger(\delta_s-\delta_t).
\]
For subsets \(S,T\subseteq V\), let \(\sigma(S,T)\) be the total leverage score of edges crossing between \(S\) and \(T\). Since the graph is undirected and the quadratic form defining \(\tau(e)\) is symmetric in the two endpoints, we have
\[
\sigma(S,T)=\sigma(T,S).
\]
This symmetry will be used to control how much leverage interaction a newly added vertex has with the current set.

The construction maintains two invariants while growing \(S\). The first says that the cross-leverage quantity between \(S\) and its complement remains controlled. The second says that a modified upper barrier function of \(M L_S M\) remains controlled. If the eigenvalues of a positive semidefinite matrix \(A\) are \(\lambda_1\ge\cdots\ge\lambda_n\), define a barrier expression of the form
\[
\Phi^u_r(A)=\sum_{i=1}^r\frac{1}{u-\lambda_i(A)},
\]
where \(r\) is a rank parameter and \(u\) is a moving upper barrier. As long as \(\Phi^u_r(A)\) is finite and controlled, the largest eigenvalue of \(A\) must lie below \(u\).

The main point is a one-step extension lemma. Suppose \(S\) satisfies the two invariants and has not yet reached the desired size. We show that some vertex \(v\notin S\) can be added while preserving both invariants.

First, more than half of the vertices outside \(S\) have small leverage interaction with \(S\). This follows by summing the interaction quantity over all \(v\notin S\). The total sum is bounded by the trace of a normalized Laplacian expression, which is controlled by rank and leverage-score identities. Since the average is small, more than half of the candidate vertices must be good for the leverage invariant.

Second, at least half of the vertices outside \(S\) are good for the barrier invariant. Adding a vertex changes \(L_S\) by the Laplacian contribution of the edges from the new vertex to the old set, and after normalization this is a positive semidefinite update. Ky Fan's maximum principle and the subadditivity of sums of top eigenvalues show that the average increase of the modified barrier over all possible added vertices is controlled. A Sherman--Morrison--Woodbury type calculation gives the precise change in \(\Phi^u_r\) under a positive semidefinite update. The conclusion is that at least half of the possible vertices do not increase the barrier beyond the allowed threshold.

Since more than half of the remaining vertices are good for the leverage invariant and at least half are good for the barrier invariant, the two good sets intersect. Choose \(v\) in this intersection and replace \(S\) by \(S\cup\{v\}\). Both invariants are preserved.

Start with a singleton set \(S\). Then \(L_S=0\), so both invariants are immediate. Apply the one-step extension lemma repeatedly until
\[
|S|\ge \frac{\varepsilon n}{42}.
\]
The invariants hold throughout the construction. At the final stage, the barrier invariant implies that every eigenvalue of \(M L_S M\) is at most \(\varepsilon\) on the image of \(L\). Hence
\[
M L_S M\preceq \varepsilon P.
\]
Conjugating back by the pseudoinverse square root gives
\[
L_S\preceq \varepsilon L.
\]
Thus the constructed set \(S\) has the required size and is \(\varepsilon\)-light.
